conversations.title: Lesson 44: Modal Auxiliaries - Introduction
conversations.content:
Modal auxiliaries (10 total): Shall, will, should, would, can, could, may, might, must, ought to
Rules:
1. Modal auxiliaries are never preceded by 'to'
2. They can only be followed by an infinitive without 'to'
3. Interrogative form is made by simple inversion of subject
4. They never take 's' at the third person singular
Must: obligation
Equivalent forms: to have to, to be obliged to, to be supposed to, to be to, need to
Examples:
I must go now
She must be my sweet
Must you work today?
I have to sleep because I am tired
We have to come tomorrow on time
They had to dance because the music was fantastic

conversations.title: Lesson 45: Can and Could

Can and Could - expresses ability, permission, suggestion, probability
Can - present / Could - past and conditional
Examples:
I can drive your car (ability)
Can I borrow your book? (permission)
We can go to cinema (suggestion)
He can win the game (probability)
Equivalent: to be able to
Examples with 'to be able to':
I will be able to speak English one day
I am able to come on Sunday
She was not able to succeed at her exam

conversations.title: Lesson 46: May and Might
conversations.content:
May and Might - express permission and probability
Might is the past and conditional form
Examples:
May I borrow your book? (permission)
He may win the game (probability)
Equivalent: to be allowed to
Examples with 'to be allowed to':
You will be allowed to come
He was allowed to attend your course
I am allowed to visit the prisoner
We are allowed to cover the event
She is allowed to come here at any time

conversations.title: Lesson 57: Comparative and Superlative - Comparative of Superiority
conversations.content:
Comparative of superiority - short adjectives: add 'er'
Snap is taller than Uche
Comparative of superiority - long adjectives: 'more' before adjective
Hopeman is more intelligent than Oy
Comparative of equality: as...as / so...as
King is as tall as Joe
King is so tall as Joe
Comparative of inequality: not as...as / not so...as
Chaï is not as fat as Faï
Ivouba is not so fat as Gloria
Comparative of inferiority: less...than
Ted is less tall than Peter
Irregular comparatives:
Good -> Better
Bad -> Worse
Many/Much -> More
Little -> Less
Far -> Farther

conversations.title: Lesson 58: Double Comparatives and Superlatives
conversations.content:
Double comparative of superiority - short adjectives: adj+er + and + adj+er
It is colder and colder
Life becomes harder and harder
Double comparative of superiority - long adjectives: more and more + adj long
This novel becomes more and more interesting
Double comparative of inferiority: less and less
This book becomes less and less important
Accidents become less and less frequent
Superlative of superiority - short adjectives: 'the' + adj+est
Snap is the smallest in the class-room
Superlative of superiority - long adjectives: the most + adj long
Dal is the most beautiful girl in their family
Superlative of inferiority: the least
This team is the least dangerous
Irregular superlatives:
Good -> The best
Bad -> The worst
Many/Much -> The most
Little -> The least
Far -> The farthest

conversations.title: Lesson 59: Passive Voice
conversations.content:
Formation: To be + Past participle of a verb + by
Active: My brother gives me money
Passive: This money is given to me by my brother
Active: The cat eats the mouse
Passive: The mouse is eaten by the cat
Active: The police has arrested the thief
Passive: The thief has been arrested by the police
Active: They had opened the windows
Passive: The windows had been opened by them
Active: He is selling interesting books
Passive: Interesting books are being sold by him
When the agent isn't known (translated by 'On' in French):
Active: Someone cleans the room everyday
Passive: The room is cleaned everyday
Active: Someone sells beer in the bottles
Passive: Beer is sold in the bottles
Active: Someone speaks English here
Passive: English is spoken here
Active: Someone is repairing my car
Passive: My car is being repaired
Active: Somebody is pulling down the house
Passive: The house is being pulled down

conversations.title: Lesson 60: Translation of 'On' in English
conversations.content:
We eat cassava in Congo
We don't eat dogs in Congo
We don't say 'maman' in English but we say 'mummy'

conversations.title: Lesson 61: Grammar - Translation of 'En' with Present Participle
conversations.content:
By + verb + ing:
He succeeded by working hard
Simultaneous actions - while/when/on + verb + ing:
She is dancing while eating
Pupils are reading while waiting for their teacher to come
On + verb + ing:
On coming home, he started writing
Manner - verb+ing:
He went to sleep crying
Verb particles:
He went out (walking)
He run out (running)
He drove away (driving)
He swam across the river (swimming)
He walked through the forest
He drove through the forest
He ran through the forest

conversations.title: Lesson 62: Adverbs of Manner and Frequency
conversations.content:
Adverbs of manner - say how something happens - go after verbs:
She sings beautifully
She danced happily
We have to think quickly
Adverbs of manner in mid-position:
My brother completely forgot my birthday
They illegally arrested him
Frequency adverbs - say how often something happens - go in mid-position (after auxiliary verbs and before verbs)
Auxiliary verb + Adverb:
I have never seen a whale
You can always come and stay with us if you want to
Have you ever played American Football?
Adverb + verb:
We usually go to Scotland in August
It sometimes gets very windy here
Initial position:
At times they are right
Sometimes she tells the truth

conversations.title: Lesson 63: The Gerund
conversations.content:
Definition: The gerund is that form of the verb which ends in -ING, and has the form of a noun and a verb
Examples:
Reading is my favourite pastime
Playing cards is not allowed here
No smoking
Giving is better than receiving
Seeing is believing
After certain verbs: like, dislike, start, begin, keep, stop, enjoy, prefer - verbs followed by gerund:
I like playing football
Stop crying
I dislike sleeping
After prepositions (by, from, before, for, without, of, after) - verbs take gerund:
I am fond of hoarding money
I am tired of waiting
Let me know, before leaving
After finishing, I will go straight home
I can stay all the day long without eating

conversations.title: Lesson 64: Question Tags
conversations.content:
Question tags are small questions added at the end of a sentence to:
a) Obtain confirmation of what is said:
You are from England, aren't you?
b) Ask a real question when you don't know the answer in advance:
You like football, don't you?
Rules:
- If the first sentence is affirmative, the tag is interro-negative
- If the first sentence is negative, the tag is interrogative
- Use the same auxiliary as in the sentence
- Use the same subject
Imperative at first person plural - use 'shall':
Let's dance, shall we?
Let's go to cinema, shall we?
With 'to be' at present first person singular - use 'are':
I am late, aren't I?
With 'this' or 'that' - use 'it':
That's John over there, isn't it?
This is yours, isn't it?
With 'there' at the beginning - repeat 'there' in the tag:
There's a bee in the room, isn't there?
With somebody, anybody, nobody, everybody - use 'they' in the tag:
Somebody told you the news, didn't they?
Nothing was broken, was it?
With hardly, no, never, little, nobody, scarcely, seldom - tag must be positive:
He hardly ever goes out, does he?
She seldom laughs, does she?
We have scarcely any time left, do we?
He'll never learn, will he?
You have no time right now, do you?
Nobody knows the exact value of it, do they?

conversations.title: Lesson 65: Translation of 'Il fallait que'
conversations.content:
Rule: S + Would + have + Past participle

Condition with 'if' - S + Would + have + PP + if:

conversations.title: Lesson 66: Tense Agreement
conversations.content:

If she speaks Chinese, she will go abroad
Preterit -> Conditional:

If I had money, I would give
If I had got money, I would have given him
Concordance of tenses:

He thought that it would rain
He sees that he has made a mistake
He saw that he had made a mistake
I work so hard that I am always tired
I worked so hard that I was always tired

He said that he was going to eat it

conversations.title: Lesson 67: Academic Education - Linguistics
conversations.content:
What is linguistics?
Linguistics is a scientific study of human language
What is the object of linguistics?
The object of linguistics is langue
What is the objective of linguistics?
The objective of linguistics is to describe languages or to show what is different in languages
What is the role of linguistics?
The role of linguistics is to show the universal and particular features of languages of the world or to describe the faculty or to show what is different in languages
What is the role of a linguist?
The role of linguists is to provide a fully description of a language
Who is Ferdinand De Saussure?
Ferdinand De Saussure is the father of linguistics
What did Ferdinand De Saussure do to be regarded as the father of Linguistics?
Ferdinand De Saussure was regarded as the father of Linguistics because he was the first who showed the scientific aspects of linguistics. He pointed out the object of linguistics, the goal of linguistics as well as its method

conversations.title: Lesson 68: Branches of Linguistics
conversations.content:
What are the fields or areas or branches of linguistics?
Linguistics encompasses many fields such as: Syntax, Morphology, Phonology, Phonetics, Semantics, Pragmatics, Sociolinguistics, Psycholinguistics, Computational Linguistics, Applied Linguistics
Definitions:
Phonetics: the study of speech sounds as regards their articulation, transmission or perception
Phonology: the study of the system of sounds of a particular language (study of phonemes)
Morphology: the study of the form or structure of words and their variations
Syntax: the study of how words are combined in sentences
Semantics: the study of the meaning of words and sentences
Pragmatics: the study of the speaker's meaning in use
Psycholinguistics: a branch of linguistics which studies the correlation between linguistic behavior and the psychological process thought to underlie that behaviour

conversations.content:

conversations.title: Lesson 73: Different Kinds of Nouns
conversations.content:
Five types of nouns in English:
1. Common nouns: a noun referring to a person, place, or thing in general way. Examples: King, Boy, Girl, City, Dog, Man, Table
2. Proper nouns: refers to the name of a person, place or thing. Examples: Rama, Levy, Tom, France, Madrid, Mrs. Smith
3. Collective nouns: the name of a group or collection taken as a whole. Examples: Team, jury, committee, army, group
4. Mass nouns (Material nouns): the name of metal or substance. Examples: silver, gold, copper, iron, wood. Note: Material nouns are uncountable, take singular verb, never use a/an before them
5. Countable nouns: a noun with both singular and plural form, can be counted. Examples: pen, cat, table
6. Uncountable nouns: a noun with only singular form, cannot be counted. Examples: water, bread, information

conversations.title: Lesson 74: Zero Article with Uncountable and Plural Nouns
conversations.content:
Zero article is used with:
General notions: I hate violence and theft
Material nouns: Gold is more expensive than silver
Food names: He likes tea but he prefers coffee
Human activities, sports, games, music: He dislikes tennis but he enjoys playing Rugby
Gerunds (verbs formed as nouns): Swimming
Seasons: I love winter
Colors: Blue is the colour I like
Languages: Chinese is a very easy language to learn
Illnesses: AIDS is a transmissible disease
Plural countable nouns: German cars are more expensive than French cars
Proper names: John Smith
Titles with names: President Kennedy
Geographic locations:
Continents: Africa
Singular countries: Canada, Congo, Morocco
States and regions: Texas, Cornwall
Singular islands: Sicily
Cities: London
Mountain peaks: Mount Everest
Lakes: Lake Michigan
Public places with proper names: Oxford Street
Days and months: Monday is the busiest day of the week
Institutions: to go to hospital, prison, church, school, university
Media: to watch TV
Idiomatic expressions: from beginning to end, at night, freedom of speech

conversations.content:

a piece of evidence = a piece of evidence
a piece of fruit = a piece of fruit
a piece of furniture = a piece of furniture
a piece of information = a piece of information
a piece of land = a piece of land

a piece of luck = a piece of luck
a piece of news = a piece of news
a piece of work = a piece of work
a sum of money = a sum of money
a piece of art = a piece of art
a piece of music = a piece of music
a piece of poetry = a piece of poetry
Also with plural after 'of':

a box of matches

conversations.title: Lesson 76: Prefixes and Suffixes - Prefixes
conversations.content:
Prefix 'Un' means 'not to' or the contrary:
able → unable
happy → unhappy
faithful → unfaithful
known → unknown
grateful → ungrateful
avoidable → unavoidable
Example: I am unable to comprehend your way of speaking
Prefix 'Mis' means 'badly':
understand → misunderstand
use → misuse
speak → misspeak
hear → mishear
Prefix 'Dis' means 'not to' or the contrary:
appear → disappear
obey → disobey
like → dislike
agree → disagree

Suffix 'Ness' - forms nouns from adjectives:

Suffix 'Less' - means 'without':

Suffix 'Ship' - forms nouns:

conversations.title: Lesson 81: False Friends (Faux Amis)
conversations.content:
False Friends are words that look similar but differ in meaning:
1. To abuse - to insult (not to take advantage of)
2. To accommodate - to lodge (not to prepare)
3. To achieve - to realize (not to complete)
4. Actually - in fact (not at present)
5. Caution - prudence (not guarantee)
6. Character - personage (not nature)
7. Check - control (not cheque)
8. Close - near (not closed)
9. Comprehensive - complete (not understanding)
10. Conductor - controller (not driver)
11. Consistent - coherent (not solid/thick)
12. To deceive - to trick (not to disappoint)
13. Delay - retard (not time limit)
14. To dispose - to get rid of (not to arrange)
15. Eventually - finally (not expert's report)
16. Extra - additional (not first-rate)
17. Fortunate - lucky (not wealthy)
18. Gentle - kind, soft (not nice)
19. Habit - habit (not dress/clothes)
20. Medicine - medication (not doctor)
21. Mercy - mercy (not thanks)
22. Petrol - gasoline (not petroleum)
23. Photograph - photograph (not photographer)
24. Phrase - expression (not sentence)
25. To regard - to consider (not to look at)
26. Relieve - to relieve (not to raise)
27. To resume - to restart (not to sum up)
28. Route - itinerary (not road)
29. Rude - rude (not sensitive)
30. Sensible - reasonable (not sensitive)
31. To survey - to examine (not to supervise)
32. To supply - to provide (not to implore)
33. To sympathetic - compassionate (not nice/friendly)

conversations.title: Lesson 82: Inversion Rules
conversations.content:
Definition: Inversion is a shift of subject and verb position where the verb takes the position of the subject.
Rule 1: Inversion is used when negative words are placed at the beginning of a clause for emphasis: Not only, not until, not once, at no time, no way, by no means, nowhere, never, seldom, rarely, scarcely, no sooner
Examples:
Nowhere in the world will Lingala be spoken in business transaction
Never in my life will I smoke
Not only did the company lose profits but also it closed
Not once was he in time
Rule 2: When expressions begin with 'only' at the beginning of a sentence, invert subject and verb: Only (in, at, by), Only once, Only recently
Example: Only in working hard can you become rich. Only recently did he inform about the case
Rule 3: When 'only if', 'only until', 'only because', 'only after', 'only when' begin a sentence, invert subject and verb of the second clause
Example: Only you are satisfied, can we place a new order?
Rule 4: When a clause begins with 'so + adjective/participle', invert subject and verb of the first clause
Examples:
So important is English today that many people want to learn it
So confusing was the map that we got lost
Rule 5: When expressions of place occur at the beginning of a sentence, invert subject and verb
Examples:
Behind is a stockyard
First came a police car, then came an ambulance

conversations.title: Lesson 83: Verbs + To + Gerund
conversations.content:
Advanced rules for verbs followed by 'to' + gerund:
1. With a view to doing something
2. When it comes to doing something
3. To own to doing = to admit doing something
4. To confess to doing = to admit something you feel ashamed about
5. To be up to doing something = to be capable of doing something
6. To stand up to doing something = to resist doing something
7. To pay attention to doing something
8. To feel up to doing something = to have the strength and energy to do something
9. To look forward to = to be thinking with pleasure about something that will happen
10. To adjust to doing something = to adapt to something
11. To be close to doing something = to almost do something
12. To commit oneself to doing something
13. To object to doing something = to refuse to do something
14. To apply oneself to doing something
15. To fall to doing something = to begin to do something
16. To be used to doing something
17. To be accustomed to doing something

conversations.title: Lesson 84: Short Answers with So
conversations.content:
Using 'so' for short answers meaning 'me too', 'him too', etc.:
I am hot. So am I. (Me too)
I have forgotten her name. So have I. (Me too)
My girlfriend works in Oyo. So does my sister. (My sister too)

conversations.title: Lesson 85: Either and Neither
conversations.content:
Either...or = either one or the other:
She is either German or Swedish
Either he is late or I've made a mistake about the time
Either replaces 'too' in negative sentences meaning 'not either':
The TV isn't working. The phone isn't working either
I don't like hockey, and I don't like rugby either
Neither...nor = neither one nor the other:
Neither James nor Antonia wants to come
I like neither beer nor whisky
Neither/nor for short answers meaning 'me neither', 'him neither':
I have never been abroad. Neither have I (or Nor have I)
I can't dance. Neither can I (or Nor can I)
I haven't been on holiday this year. Neither have I

conversations.title: Lesson 86: Used To, Be Used To, Get Used To
conversations.content:
Used to + verb - expresses past habits or past situations different from present:
I used to smoke cigarette; now I smoke a pipe
I used to wet the bed when I was a little boy
I used to tease my mum and dad in my childhood
He used to drink beer; now he drinks wine
To be used to + verb+ing = to be accustomed to:
To get used to + verb+ing = to become accustomed to
To become used to = to become accustomed to
Examples:
I am used to noise
You will soon get used to the electric typewriters
They soon got used to the traffic regulations
They soon got used to driving on the left
I am used to working in a noisy place
You will soon get used to typing on electric typewriters
She became used to smoking by dint of trying

conversations.title: Lesson 87: Adjectives - Placement and Agreement
conversations.content:
Attributive adjectives are placed before the noun:
A wild animal, a very expensive pen, a more interesting film, the most difficult problem
Adjectives never take 's' in plural:
Wild animals, we were ill
Order of multiple adjectives:
Color, origin, material, function generally in that order:
Black Spanish leather boot
Other adjectives precede them: A big white dog, A well-known Japanese writer
Using 'and' with adjectives:
A dirty, torn coat (no 'and')
A practical economical car (no 'and')
A concrete and glass building (with 'and' for different parts)
Red and yellow socks (with 'and')
Adjectives ending in 'ly': Friendly and lovely are adjectives, not adverbs
A friendly letter, a lovely view
Adverbs from these: 'in a friendly way'
Compound adjectives:
Adjective+noun+ed: dark-haired, blue-eyed, old-fashioned, bad-tempered, narrow-minded, middle-aged, absent-minded, short-sighted, left-handed
Noun/adverb/adjective+present participle: tennis-playing, fast-talking, nice-looking
Adjective+adjective (color): red-brown, grey-green, blue-green, grey-white
Substantivized adjectives: the blind, the deaf, the poor, the rich, the wealthy, the well-off

conversations.title: Lesson 88: Few vs A Few, Little vs A Little
conversations.content:
Few = not many, not enough - used with countable nouns (plural) - negative meaning:
Astronomy is a hard subject that few students study
Few persons came to attend the course, it rained a lot
A few = some, enough, a number of - used with countable nouns (plural) - positive meaning:
English is an easy language to learn, a few students are interested in it
If you have no money, you have few friends; but if you have money you have a few friends
Little = not much, not enough - used with uncountable nouns (singular) - negative meaning:
I have little money with me; I am unable to afford this laptop
We are able to do this exercise; we need little help from you
A little = some, enough - used with uncountable nouns (singular) - positive meaning:
I am stuck; I need a little money to afford it
It requires a little effort to get it
She has worked hard, so Sarah deserves a little money
Aunty is no longer able to do it, she needs a little help

conversations.title: Lesson 89: Each and Every
conversations.content:
Each - thinks of things separately, one by one - more usual for small numbers:
Study each sentence carefully
There were four books on the table. Each book was a different colour
At the beginning of the game, each player has three cards
Every - thinks of things as a group (similar to 'all') - more usual for large numbers:
Every sentence must have a verb
Carol has read every book in the library
I would like to visit every country in the world
Each (not every) can be used for two things:
In a football match, each team has 11 players (not every team)
Every (not each) to say how often something happens:
How often do you go shopping? Every day (not each day)
There's a bus every ten minutes (not each ten minutes)
Each can be used alone (without noun) or with 'one':
None of the rooms was the same. Each was different / Each one was different
Each of + the/these/etc.: Read each of these sentences carefully
Each of us/you/them: Each of them is a different colour
Every one (but not 'every' alone): Have you read all these books? Yes, every one
Every one of (not 'every of'): I've read every one of those books
Each in the middle or end of sentence:
The students were each given a book
These oranges cost 25 pence each

conversations.title: Lesson 90: Summary of Verb Agreement
conversations.content:
1. With titles of books/journals, organizations - verb is singular:
Things Fall Apart deals with traditional African life
2. With 'one of the + plural noun + who/that/which' - verb is plural (agrees with antecedent plural noun):
Percides is one of the ladies who are stubborn
Lepari is one of the boys who have much money
3. With 'one of the + plural noun' alone - verb is singular:
One of the girls is here. One of the students was killed
4a. Neither + singular noun + nor + singular noun / Either + singular noun or + singular noun / Not only + singular noun + but also + singular noun - verb is singular:
Neither the teacher nor the learner is right
4b. Neither + plural noun + nor + plural noun / Either + plural noun or + plural noun / Not only + plural noun + but also + plural noun - verb is plural:
Neither the girls nor the boys are right
5. With 'as well as', 'together with', 'along with' - verb agrees with the first noun:
Congo, as well as ten countries, is not rich
Boys, as well as girls, like eating
6. With 'a list of', 'amount of', 'hours', 'kilos', 'each', 'every' - verb is singular:
Forty hours is enough for me. Six kilometres is sufficient
With 'a number of', 'all' - verb is plural:
A number of children were killed in South Africa
7. With 'neither of', 'either of', 'any of', 'a lot of' - verb can be singular or plural:
Neither of my friends is/are harlot

conversations.title: Lesson 91: Some, Any and Their Compounds
conversations.content:
Some - used in affirmative sentences:
Opi has some problems. I have some fish. You have some mangoes
Any - used in interrogative and negative sentences:
Do you have any money to give me? I don't have any money to give you
Any can also mean 'any' as in 'any pen, any book'
No - partial negation:
We have got no money
Compounds of some, any, no:
Someone / Anyone = somebody / anybody = someone
No one = nobody = no person
Something / Anything = something
Nothing = nothing
Nowhere = nowhere
Somewhere / Anywhere = somewhere
Sometimes / At times = sometimes

conversations.title: Lesson 92: Gender in English
conversations.content:
Gender refers to ways of talking about men and women inclusively.
Human race:
Instead of 'man' and 'mankind' for all humans, use: humanity, the human race, human beings, or people
Pronouns with indefinite pronouns:
After everybody, everyone, anybody, anyone, somebody, someone - use plural pronouns they, them, their:
Does everybody know what they want?
Somebody has left their coat here
I hope nobody has forgotten to bring their passport with them
Everybody says that they don't have money
In informal language, 'he', 'him', 'his' is also used (masculine dominates feminine):
Everybody says that he does not have money
A person has brought his bag with him

conversations.title: Lesson 93: Clauses and Phrases
conversations.content:
Clause - a group of words that includes a subject and a verb and forms a sentence or part of a sentence:
They often go to Italy because they love their food
Main clause (independent clause / matrix clause): can form a sentence alone
Subordinate clause (dependent clause / embedded clause): adds information but cannot stand alone
Example: She answered the phone when it rang
Coordinate clause: two or more parts joined by and, or, but with equal importance
Phrase - a group of words with a particular meaning when used together, makes sense but not complete sense:
The sun rises in the east
The green car
On Friday morning
Types of phrases:
Noun phrase - head is a noun: The woman who lives here, The dog which barks every morning
Verb phrase - head is a verb: sells at the corner of my house, jogs every morning
Prepositional phrase - head is a preposition: on the table, in my bedroom
Adjectival phrase - head is an adjective: beautiful girl, handsome boy
Adverbial phrase - head is an adverb: extremely excited, very good

conversations.title: Lesson 94: Phrasal Verbs
conversations.content:
Phrasal verbs are verbs used with prepositions that change meaning depending on the preposition.
Separable phrasal verbs - you can place a pronoun or noun between verb and preposition:
At the conference, scientists handed out brochures / handed brochures out
With personal pronouns, the pronoun goes between verb and preposition: scientists handed them out
Common separable phrasal verbs:
To ask out, to bring about, to bring up, to call back, to call up, to check out, to cheer up, to clean up, to cross out, to cut out, to drop off, to figure out, to fill out, to find out, to get back, to give up, to hand in, to hang up, to keep out, to kick out, to look over, to look up, to make up, to pass out, to pick out, to pick up, to point out, to put away, to put back, to put off, to put on, to put out, to shut off, to take off, to take out, to take over, to take up, to tear up, to think over, to throw away, to turn down, to turn in, to turn off, to turn out, to turn up
Inseparable phrasal verbs - must be directly followed by preposition; no word between verb and preposition:
Common inseparable phrasal verbs:
To call on, to catch up (with), to check in, to come across, to drop by, to drop out, to get along, to get in, to get off, to get out of, to get over, to go over, to grow up, to keep up, to look after, to look out, to pass away, to put up with, to show up
Examples:
The worst damage will not show up until years after the accident
It's hard to keep up with technological advances
I come across people who look after wild animals

conversations.title: Lesson 95: Different Kinds of Verbs
conversations.content:
Six classes of verbs:
1. Intransitive verbs - do not require an object:
A baby cries. A baby snores very loudly at night
2. Transitive verbs - require an object:
Mr Obama rules the USA
3. Di-transitive verbs - have two objects (direct object and indirect object):
The teachers explain students lessons
The order can be altered with preposition: The teachers explain lessons to students
Common di-transitive verbs: to give, to bring, to hand, to offer, to promise
4. Intensive verbs - describe state of being, complete with complement (AP, NP, PP):
Mrs Nkara is shy. Mrs Nkara becomes shy
5. Complex transitive verbs - take two objects (DO and Object Complement) - order cannot be changed:
Americans elected Obama president
Common complex-transitive verbs: to name, to appoint, to call, to elect, to make, to find, to imagine, to vote
Example: FIFA voted Pele sportsman of the century
6. Prepositional verbs - select a prepositional object:
Parents glanced at the photos

conversations.title: Lesson 96: Intensifiers - How + Adjective/Adverb
conversations.content:
Structure: How + adj/adv + to be + subject + complement?
How fast can you type?
How worried are you about the situation in Congo?
How likely is that to happen?
How early would you like to be woken?
How long is the rope?
How long do you want it?
How quickly do you want something to be done?
How fast can he run?
How often do you go swimming?
How late will you stay?
How much money have you got?

conversations.title: Lesson 97: Figures of Speech
conversations.content:
Figures of speech - ways of using language to convey meaning beyond literal meaning:
1. Metaphor
2. Metonymy
3. Allegory
4. Personification
5. Pathetic fallacy
6. Alliteration
7. Assonance
8. Onomatopoeia
Other stylistic effects:
Irony, Hyperbole, Oxymoron, Paradox
Poetry types:
Lyric poetry - fairly short, expresses thoughts and feelings
Epic poetry - much longer, deals with action of great men and women or history of nations
Poem

conversations.title: Lesson 98: Verbalizing Mathematical Language - Addition and Subtraction
conversations.content:
Addition (+) -
4 + 3:
Four plus three
Four and three
Four added to three
Add three to four
Sum (solution to an addition problem)
4 + 3 = 7:
Four plus three equals seven
The sum of four and three is seven
Subtraction (-) -
9 - 5:
Nine minus five
Nine take away five
Nine less five
Subtract five from nine
Difference (solution to a subtraction problem)
9 - 5 = 4:
Nine minus five equals four
The difference of nine and five is four

conversations.title: Lesson 99: Verbalizing Mathematical Language - Multiplication and Division
conversations.content:
Multiplication (x) -
5 x 6:
Five times six
Five multiplied by six
The product of five and six
Multiply five by six
Product (solution to a multiplication problem)
5 x 6 = 30:
Five times six equals thirty
The product of five and six is thirty
Division (÷) -
20 ÷ 5:
Twenty divided by five
Divide twenty by five
Five into twenty
Twenty over five (20/5)
Quotient (solution to a division problem)
20 ÷ 5 = 4:
Twenty divided by five equals four
The quotient of twenty divided by five is four
Five goes into twenty four times

conversations.title: Lesson 100: Verbalizing Mathematical Language - Equals, Fractions, Percent, Ratio
conversations.content:
Equal (=) -
6 + 4 = 10:
Six plus four equals ten
Six plus four is equal to ten
Six plus four is ten
Six plus four makes ten
Six plus four gives you ten
Six plus four yields ten
Plus or minus (±) -
±5 minutes: plus or minus five minutes / give or take five minutes
±5°: accurate within plus or minus five degrees
Fractions (a/b) -
1/4: one-fourth, one quarter, one over four
2/3: two-thirds
4/7: four-sevenths
5 3/16: five and three sixteenths
a/b: a over b
Percent (%) -
75%: seventy-five percent
Ratio (a:b) -
1:8: one to eight ratio / ratio of one to eight
Decimal (.) -
4.3: four-point three / four and three-tenths
6.27: six point two seven / six point twenty-seven / six and twenty-seven hundredths
3.667: three point six six seven
Negative values (-) -
5 - 7 = -2: five minus seven equals negative two
-10°F: minus ten degrees Fahrenheit / ten degrees below zero / ten below
Exponential values (xn) -
4² = 4 x 4: four squared
7³ = 7 x 7 x 7: seven cubed
8⁵ = 8 x 8 x 8 x 8 x 8: eight (raised) to the fifth power / eight to the fifth

conversations.title: Lesson 101: Metric Measures
conversations.content:
Length -
10 millimetres (mm) = 1 centimetre (cm) = 0.394 inch
100 centimetres = 1 metre (m) = 39.4 inches / 1.094 yards
1000 metres = 1 kilometre (km) = 0.6214 mile
The basic unit of length is the metre
Area -
100 square metres (m²) = 1 are (a) = 0.025 acre
100 ares = 1 hectare (ha) = 2.471 acres
100 hectares = 1 square kilometre (km²) = 0.386 square mile
Weight or Mass -
1000 milligrams (mg) = 1 gram (g) = 15.43 grains
1000 grams (g) = 1 kilogram (kg) = 2.205 pounds
1000 kilograms = 1 tonne (t) = 19.688 hundredweight
The basic unit of weight or mass is the kilogram
Capacity -
10 millilitres (ml) = 1 centilitre = 0.018 pint (0.021 US pint)
100 centilitres (cl) = 1 litre (l) = 1.76 pints (2.1 US pint)
10 litres (l) = 1 decalitre (dal) = 2.2 gallons (2.63 US gallons)
The basic unit of capacity is the litre

conversations.title: Lesson 102: Non-Metric Measures
conversations.content:
Length:
1 inch (in) = 25.4 millimetres
12 inches = 1 foot (ft) = 30.48 centimetres
3 feet = 1 yard (yd) = 0.914 metre
220 yards = 1 furlong = 201.17 metres
8 furlongs = 1 mile = 1.609 kilometres
1760 yards = 1 mile = 1.609 kilometres
Area:
1 square (sq) inch = 6.452 sq centimetres (cm2)
144 sq inches = 1 sq foot = 929.03 sq centimetres
9 sq feet = 1 sq yard = 0.836 sq metre
4840 sq yards = 1 acre = 0.405 hectare
640 acres = 1 sq mile = 259 hectares / 2.59 sq kilometres
Weight:
437 grains = 1 ounce (oz) = 28.35 grams
16 ounces = 1 pound (lb) = 0.454 kilogram
14 pounds = 1 stone (st) = 6.356 kilograms
8 stone = 1 hundredweight (cwt) = 50.8 kilograms
20 hundredweight = 1 ton = 1016.04 kilograms
British capacity:
20 fluid ounces (fl oz) = 1 pint (pt) = 0.568 litre
2 pints = 1 quart (qt) = 1.136 litres
8 pints = 1 gallon (gal) = 4.546 litres
American capacity:
16 US fluid ounces = 1 US pint = 0.473 litre
2 US pints = 1 US quart = 0.946 litre
4 US quarts = 1 US gallon = 3.785 litres

conversations.title: Lesson 103: Numbers in Measurement (Britain and America)
conversations.content:
Length of time:
Hours (hrs), Minutes (mins), Seconds (secs)
Examples: Cover the pan and simmer gently for one hour; He took just two minutes to knock out his opponent; The fastest time was 12 mins 26 secs
Person's height:
Feet and inches / Metres and centimetres (UK)
Examples: She's 1.63 metres tall; He's only five feet four (inches); He's only five foot four
Distance by road: Miles
Example: It is 42 miles to Liverpool; The signpost said Liverpool 42
Speed:
Miles per hour (mph), Kilometres per hour (kph), Kilometres per second
Examples: She was driving at 75 miles per hour; A speed limit of 50kph; Light travels at 299792 kilometres per second; A hundred miles an hour police chase
Distance in sport:
Metres, Yards, Miles (US)
Examples: The women's 800 metres freestyle; A six mile run
Area of land (farmland): Acres / Hectares
Examples: A house with 10 acres of grounds; A 2000-hectare farm
Regions or areas of a country:
Square miles / Square kilometres
Examples: Dartmoor covers an area of more than 350 square miles; Population density is only 24 people per square kilometre
Area of a room/garden, etc.:
Square yards/feet, square metres (UK), ....by.... (...x...)
Examples: 5000 square feet of office space; 15 square metres of carpet (5m x 3m); A carpet fifteen metres square (15m x 15m); A room sixteen feet by twelve (16ft x 12ft)
Weight of food:
Pounds and ounces / Kilograms and grams (UK)
Examples: Fold in 6 ounces of flour; 250 grams of brie please; Add half a cup of sugar
Weight of a person:
Stones and pounds / Pounds only (US) / Kilograms (UK)
Examples: She weighs 8st 10 lb; My brother weighs 183 pounds
Weight of a baby:
Pounds and ounces / Kilograms (UK)
Example: The baby weighed 6lb 4oz at birth
Heavy item/large amounts:
Tons/tonnes, pounds, kilograms (UK)
Examples: The price of copper fell by 11 a tonne; A car packed with 140 pounds of explosive; A 40kg sack of gravel; Our baggage allowance is only 20 kilos
Milk:
Pints/half pints (UK), Pints/quarts/gallons (US)
Examples: A one-pint carton of milk; A quart of milk
Beer: Pints/half pints (US) - Example: A half of lager please (=half a pint)
Wine, bottled drinks: Litres/centilitres - Example: A litre of juice
Other liquids:
Litres (UK), Fluid ounces/gallon, Millilitres (scientific context)
Examples: Half a litre of cooking oil; 5 litres of paint; 2 gallons of paint; 100ml sulphuric acid
Liquids in cooking: Fluid ounces / Millilitres (UK) - Example: Add 8 fl oz milk and beat thoroughly
Petrol (BrE) / Gasoline/Diesel (US): Gallons (US), Litres (UK) - Example: My new car does over 50 miles to the gallon

conversations.title: Lesson 104: Formal Letter Writing (Applying for a Job - British Style)
conversations.content:
Format:
Your address (top right):
22 Rocks Lane
Bristol BS8 9DF
Date: 20 April 2005
Recipient address:
Ms Patricia Wright
Personnel Department
Multimedia Design
4 Albion Road
London SE1 8DD
Dear Ms Wright,
Paragraph 1: Explain which job you are applying for and how/where you heard about it
I am writing to apply for the post of assistant designer advertised in the Evening Post of 18 April. Please find enclosed a copy of my CV.
Paragraph 2: Briefly describe your most relevant qualifications and/or experience
Since graduating from Cardiff University, I have been working for EMS Corporate Imaging on a contract basis. I have become particularly interested in interactive and multimedia work and now wish to develop my career in that direction.
Paragraph 3: Explain why you want the job and why you think you would be good at it
I would welcome the chance to work as part of a small dynamic team where I could make a significant contribution while developing my skills yet further. I would be happy to show you a portfolio of my work.
Paragraph 4: Say how you can be contacted and/or when you are available for interview
I am available for interview next week and look forward to hearing from you.
Yours sincerely,
Mark Wallace
Enc. CV

conversations.title: Lesson 105: Useful Phrases for a Job Application
conversations.content:
Paragraph 1:
I noted with interest your advertisement for a ... in today's edition of...
I am writing in response to your advertisement in... for the position of...
I would like to apply for the vacancy advertised in...
With reference to your advertisement in...
I am interested in applying for the post of...
As you will see from my CV...
I have enclosed a copy of my CV, from which you will see...
Please find enclosed a copy of my CV.
Paragraph 2:
I am currently studying... at...
After graduating from..., I...
Since leaving university, I have...
On leaving school, I...
Having gained a degree, I...
While I was working at...
During my employment at...
I am currently employed as...
Paragraph 3:
This post interests me because...
I would welcome the chance to gain more experience of...
I would be grateful for the opportunity to improve my ... skills
I have extensive experience of...
Paragraph 4:
If you consider that my experience and qualifications are suitable...
I am available for interview any afternoon and would be pleased to discuss the post in person.
I will be available for interview from... to...
I can arrange to attend an interview whenever convenient for you.

conversations.content:

conversations.title: Lesson 107: The Secret of English - Expressions for Working Hard
conversations.content:
To work hard = to work flat out = to work like a devil = to slave away = to hammer away
Example: I must work hard
To be like a million dollars = to feel like a million bucks = to feel at ease, to feel well
Example: I feel like a million dollars
To go smoothly = to go swimmingly = to go hunky-dory = to go well
Examples:
How are you? Everything is going smoothly / Everything is going swimmingly / Everything is going hunky-dory
Different words for 'name':
What is your name? What is your lastname? What is your familyname? What is your surname? What is your Christian name? What is your given name? What is your firstname? What is your nickname?
Getting attention:
Please Sir! Excuse me. Silence please. Listen to me! Follow me! Pin your ears back! Be attentive. Look at me!

conversations.title: Lesson 108: Classroom Expressions - Speaking and Instructions
conversations.content:
Speak up! Speak aloud! Speak it out! Say it out! Don't speak quickly. Speak slowly. Could you speak more slowly please? Could you rephrase your English please?
Get out of:
Get out of my sight. Get out of my house. Stay away from my daughter.
Let's + verb:
Let's roll! Let's begin! Let's start! Let's sing now! Let's go home! Let's go back home.
Returning to the subject:
Let's go back to the matter in hand. Let's go back to our subject. Let's get down to the basics. To get back to basics.
At your disposal:
I am at your disposal. I am at your service.
Talkative person:
A talkative person, a garrulous person, a chatter-box, verbose, verbose speaker. Example: You are talkative.

conversations.title: Lesson 109: Expressions for Waiting, Sitting, Standing and Continuing
conversations.content:
Wait expressions:
Hold on! Hang on. Hold on a sec. Hang on a sec. Wait a minute. Wait a jiffy. Wait a little bit.
Stand up and sit down:
Stand up! Sit down. Be seated. Remain seated. Have a seat. Take a seat.
And you?:
And you? What about you? How about you?
Continue doing something:
Carry on speaking. Go on doing the same thing. Keep on dancing. Keep working in the same way.
Stop doing something:
Stop making noise! Stop talking rubbish. Stop talking bullshit. Stop kidding. Stop joking. Stop teasing.
I am kidding / joking / teasing / playing.
Mistake:
It is a big mistake. You made a mistake.
Be quiet:
Keep quiet. Be quiet! Keep cool! Keep calm.
Don't show off:
Don't show off. Don't boast yourself. Don't brag. Don't pride yourself. Don't blow your own trumpet.
Be careful / watchful.
Shut your mouth! Shut up! Shut your gob. Shut your trap.
Get out! Go out! Get in! Come in!
You block my view. You're in my way.
Be quick! Hurry up! Look sharp. Look alive.
To be in a rush = to be in a hurry. Example: I am in a rush. I am in a hurry.

conversations.title: Lesson 110: Describing People - Liar, Joker, Beggar, Swindler
conversations.content:
You are a liar. You are a joker. You are a beggar. You are a swindler.
Go on! Come on! Go ahead. Be my guest!
Do you understand?:
Do you understand? Do I make myself understood? Do I make myself heard? Do you get a clue? Do you get me right? Do you get the picture? Do you get the point? Do you figure it out?
I don't catch you. I don't get you. I don't understand you. I don't twig you.
I don't know. I have no idea. I have no more ideas.
I am confused = I am fuddled = I am gabled = I am muddled = I am woolly-wooly = I am woolly-headed.
I am stuck = I am lost.
I am unable. I am able to do it.
I am a beginner = I am a novice = I am a learner = I am a newbie. He is a rookie.
I am free = I am available.

conversations.title: Lesson 111: Vocabulary - Healer, Witch, Wizard, Speaking English Well
conversations.content:
A herbalist = A native doctor = a healer
An occultist = A juju maker = a witch doctor
A witch (female) / A wizard (male)
To bewitch / To heal
Don't speak anyhow. You speak anyhow. Don't walk anyhow, you are already grown up.
You speak the Queen's English = You speak the King's English = You speak excellent English = You speak good English = You speak English perfectly = You speak English flawlessly = You speak English impeccably = You speak English properly = You speak English correctly.
English is spoken around the world = across the world = all over the world = everywhere in the world = throughout the world = worldwide.
English is the Universal language = the International language = the Worldwide language = the most useful language.

conversations.title: Lesson 112: Expressions - To have other fish to fry, To be busy, To be at ease
conversations.content:
To have other fish to fry = to have other things to do
To be busy = to be hectic = manic = active
I am at ease. Relax. Make yourself at home. Feel comfortable = Feel cosy.
Be it in... be it in... = whether in... or in...
In any field of study: Be it in politics, be it in economics, etc.
Who can resolve it? Who can solve it? Who can do it? Who is able to do it? Who has a question?
I can do it in less than no time = I can do it in double quick time.
Congratulations! = Congrats! I congratulate you.
Don't be shy = Don't be coy = Don't be timid = Don't be bashful = Don't be sheepish = Don't be inhibited. Come out of your shell.
To be ashamed. Shame on you! It is shameful. You are shameless.
Don't grumble = Don't complain = Don't worry! = Don't worry yourself about it! = Take it easy!
Whether you like it or not = Believe it or not = Like it or not.
Have you finished? = Are you done? = Are you through?
I have finished = I am done = I am through. Not yet. I haven't yet finished.
It is overhere = It is here. It is there = It is over there.
It is over = That's all. That's all for today. Let's call it a day.
Thank you: Ta! Thanks! Cheers! Thank you! Thanks a bunch! Thanks a lot! Thank you very much.
You're welcome: Never mind. Don't mention it.
It doesn't matter = That's no matter. Never mind.
Good luck! Break a leg!
Enjoy! Enjoy your meal!
Wishes: All the best. I wish you all the best. May God take control of you. God bless you! Greet your family for me.
Have a good day! Have a good night! Have a good party! Have good dreams! Sweet dreams.
Take care = Take care of yourself. Let's keep in touch.
I hope that = Hopefully. Example: Hopefully, we will meet shortly.
Come first. Eat first. Just eat. Just do it.
Luck you! = Happy you!

conversations.title: Lesson 113: Expressions - Going, Leaving, At vs To, I don't care
conversations.content:
Where are you off to? I am going now. I am off. I am going back home. I am going home now. I am leaving now. I must leave you now.
To go abroad, to go somewhere, to go downtown, to go home.
Where are you going now? I am going to buy something.
At vs To:
At (no movement): I am at home, I am at church, I am at school, I am at the center, I am at work, I am at mother's place
To (movement): I am going to church, I am going to school, I am going to work, I am going to the house
Exceptions: I am going home, I am going downtown, I am going to my friend's place
I don't care = I don't care about it = I don't give a shit about it = I don't give a damn about it.
5 out of 5 = 5/5. Ten out of ten = 10/10. To have got twenty out of twenty. I have got twenty out of twenty.
To be off base = to be out of topic = to be out of point.
I am tired of it = I am fed up = I am pissed off. Enough is enough.
To be in fashion = to be in fashionable = to be in vogue.
To be up to date.
To be outdated = to be out of date = to be out of fashion = to be old-fashioned.
Be you rich or poor, you will die. Be it good or bad, I will do it. Be she beautiful or ugly, I will love her. Be you a girl or boy, we're all equal.
Is it right or wrong? Is it true or false?
You drive me crazy = You drive me wild. You attract me. You magnetize me.
You look tired = You look sad = You look shy.
Be polite, be faithful, be obedient, be kind, be on time, be quick, be quiet, be curious, be watchful, be short.

conversations.title: Lesson 114: Expressions - Surprise, Madness, Crazy about, Allows us to
conversations.content:
To be surprised, to be astonished, to be amazed, to be marvelled, to be taken aback, to be flabbergasted, to be astounded, to be thunderstruck, to be zoned out, to be zoomed out.
To be potty = to be dippy = to be crazy = to be mad = to be maniac = to be drongo = to be loony = to be loco.
To go bananas.
Lunacy = madness.
To be mad for somebody/something = to be crazy about somebody/something. Example: I am crazy about English.
It allows us to travel a lot. It allows me to be connected with the outside world. It allows us to be free. It allows us to feel at ease.
It enables us to become someone. It enables us to be known. It enables us to succeed easily. It enables me to know many people. It enables them to do research.
To be thirsty of + verb+ing:
I am thirsty of drinking water. I am thirsty of learning. I am thirsty of working.
Oh my goodness! That's great! It is fabulous! It is fantastic! That's wonderful! That's marvellous! That's enjoyable! That's super! That's tremendous!
It is easy = It is a doddle = It is a cinch = It is a no brainer = It is as easy as can be = It is a piece of cake = It is straightforward = It is effortless = It is painless. Example: It is not rocket science.
This question is a no brainer. This is a no brainer problem to solve.
It is like that = It is like this.
It is promising! It looks promising! It is hopeful! It is encouraging! I promise you something.

conversations.title: Lesson 115: Expressions - Convincing, Your funeral, Bullshit, Useless, Important
conversations.content:
It is convincing = It is persuasive = It is strong = It is powerful.
That's your funeral = That's your business. That's not my business = That's not my funeral = That's none of my business. That's none of your business.
That's bullshit = That's crap = That's cobblers = That's stupidity = That's nonsense = That's garbage. You are saying stupid things.
It's useless = It's meaningless = It's pointless = It is needless = It's aimless = It's worthless = It is purposeless = It's nugatory = It is trivial = It's futile = It's duff.
It is important = It is significant = It is momentous = It is crucial = It is critical = It is vital = It is consequential = It is historic.
It is outstanding = It is fine = It is extraordinary = It is exceptional = It is excellent = It is superb = It is superlative.
It is relevant = It is pertinent = It is applicable = It is material = It is suitable = It is necessary = It is needy = It is worthy.
It is paramount = It is pre-eminent = It is highest = It is supreme = It is prime = It is primary = It is cardinal = It is utmost = It is foremost.
It is something new, something good, something important, something crucial.
That's incredible = That's unbearable = That's unbelievable = It is remarkable = It is extraordinary = It is unforgettable = It is memorable = It is staggering = It is breathtaking = It is startling = It is astonishing = It is amazing.

conversations.title: Lesson 116: Expressions - Funny, It is said, Thanks to, Aware, Depends on
conversations.content:
It is funny = It is comic = It is hilarious = It is humorous = It is humous = It is amusing = It is entertaining = It is rum = It is queer.
It is not said. Is it said in English? It is pronounced. It is written like that. It is spelt. It is transcribed. It is translated like that. It is described like that.
It is thanks to me, him, her, us, you, them, my parents.
To be aware of something:
I am aware of it. You are aware of it. He is aware of it. She is aware of it. We are aware of it. They are aware of it.
It depends on me, you, him, her, us, you, them.
It's up to me, you, him, her, us, you, them.
It is a sin in front of God if you don't respect the word of God. It is a sin in front of God if you don't honour your parents. It is a sin in front of God if you don't obey your parents. It is a sin in front of God if you disobey your parents.
I am a leader to be, a physician to be, a boss to be, a business man to be, a mother to be. As a father to be.
Still:
I am still busy. He is still at home. She is still at school. He is still at church. They are still at market. He is still at work.
It is costly = It is expensive. It costs an arm and a leg. It is cheap = It is not expensive.

conversations.title: Lesson 117: No longer / No more / Any longer / Any more
conversations.content:
No longer / No more (affirmative sentences):
I am no longer a teenager. I am no longer a child. I am no longer sick. I am no more at the center. She is no more at school. I am no more at church. He is no more at the market.
Any longer / Any more (negative sentences):
I am not a teenager any longer. I am not a child any longer. I am not a jobless any longer. He is not abroad any longer. I don't call him anymore. I don't meet him anymore. He doesn't drink wine anymore. I am not gonna talk about it anymore. Assa can't stay with us anymore. Don't do it anymore.
As for = As to:
As for me, as for you, as for her, as for him, as for us, as for you (plural), as for them.
According to (never used with 'me'):
According to you, according to him, according to her, according to us, according to you (plural), according to them.
In my view = In my opinion = For me = To the best of my knowledge = For my money = In my book = In my way of thinking.
Even:
Even me, even you, even him, even her, even us, even you (plural), even them.
The same:
The same day, the same week, the same month, the same year, the same thing, the same as you. It is the same thing. It amounts to the same thing. It comes to the same thing.

conversations.title: Lesson 118: Expressions - For the sake of, Including, More than, Let your name be
conversations.content:
For the sake of it = For the fun of it = For the heck of it = For the hell of it.
For the sake of my people. For the sake of everybody. For God's sake.
Including me, you, him, her, us, you (plural), them.
God is more than me, you, him, her, us, you (plural), them.
Let your name be glorified, magnified, sanctified, exalted, magnified.
On the behalf of + noun:
On the behalf of my country, I must work hard in order to succeed. On the behalf of my family and parents, I would like to thank you for your support.
I rely on you = I count on you. I believe in you. I trust you.
What the heck are you doing? = What the fuck are you doing? = What the hell are you doing? = What the deuce are you doing? = What on earth are you doing? = What in the world are you doing?
No matter:
No matter what, no matter who, no matter whom, no matter which, no matter where, no matter when, no matter how.
No matter what you do, I will come. No matter who you are, I will never fear you.
So ever:
Whatsoever = whatever. Whosoever = whoever. Whomsoever = whomever. Whichsoever = whichever. Whensoever = whenever. Wheresoever = wherever. Whosever.
To happen:
What is happening? What happened? What happened to you? What has happened to him? What is going on? What's wrong? What's wrong with you? What has come over to you? Who do you think you are?

conversations.title: Lesson 119: Telephone and Expressions of Regret, Boring, Free, Feel like
conversations.content:
Telephone words:
Who is speaking? Pick up your phone. Hang up your phone. Don't hang up. To switch off, to switch on. To put off, to put on.
What a pity! = What a disaster! = What a fiasco! = What a shame! Example: It is a pity for my sister to give up school.
It is boring = It is mind-numbing = It is unexciting = It is uninteresting = It is dull.
It's free = It's gratis = It's free of charge. Freely. Example: I give you this book freely.
To feel like + verb+ing:
I feel like drinking water. I feel like going back. I feel like travelling. I feel like working. I feel like sleeping. I feel like loving someone. I feel like making love.
Need:
I need your pen. I need some water. I need some money. I need a girlfriend. I need a boyfriend.
The lack of:
The lack of water, the lack of money, the lack of job, the lack of good education system.

conversations.title: Lesson 120: Lend and Borrow, Broke, Important matters, Speak your mind
conversations.content:
Lend and borrow:
Can you lend me your pen? Can I borrow your pen?
To be broke:
I am broke = I am flat broke. I am short of money = I am moneyless. To be stony broke. I am stony broke. To have a hard time. Can you give me some coins? I have nothing. I am having a hard time.
Nitty-gritty, the crux of something, the nub of something.
Let's get down to the nitty-gritty. Let's get down to the crux of the matter. Let's get down to the nub of the problem.
Speak your mind. Have your say = It is your turn.
A good deal = a great deal = a lot = much.
They spent a good deal of money. It took a great deal of time. I feel a good deal better. We see them a great deal.
Drop dead = Go to hell = Get the hell out of here = Push off = Piss off = Lay off = Sold off = Get lost = Scram = Bugger off = Fuck off = Fuck you = Get stuffed = Bug out = Leave alone = Kiss my ass = Clear off = Off you go!

conversations.title: Lesson 121: Expressions - Tired, Stupid, All of us, Between us, Each of us
conversations.content:
To be tired = To be tired up = To be weary = To be bushed = To be exhausted = To be dog-tired = To be raddled = To be dead = To be dead beat = To be washed out = To be wiped out = To be dragged out = To be fagged = To be fagged out = To be zonked = To be shagged = To be worn = To be shagged out = To be fatigued = To be shattered = To be haggard = To be drawn = To be lost = To be wrecked.
Stupid, idiot:
A dodo, asinine, brainless, bastard, birdbrain, boob, dim, dim-witted, dingbat, dopey, dozy, drongo, dull, dope, dull-witted, dullard, plonker, nanny, a ninny, dumcluck, dumb, dumbness, dumbo, dummy, dunderhead, doff, featherbrain, featherbrained, halfwitted, harebrained, jackass, lamberbrain, tomfool.
All of us, all of you, all of them.
Both of us, both of you, both of them.
Between us, between you, between them.
Among us, among you, among them.
Each one of us, each one of you, each one of them.
Each of us, each of you, each of them.
Everyone of us, everyone of you, everyone of them.
Every single one of us, every single one of you, every single one of them.

conversations.title: Lesson 122: Expressions with Dire, From... to..., Fucking
conversations.content:
How shall I put it? Just between the two of us. Between you and me. Let me say in passing. It goes without saying. Actually = In fact. Not to say. They say that. Nevertheless = Having said this. Needless to say. As they say. As you might say.
From morning to evening (British) / Through morning to evening (American)
From beginning to end / Through beginning to end
From start to finish / Through start to finish
From soup to nuts / Through soup to nuts
From 9 o'clock to 12 o'clock / Through 9 o'clock to 12 o'clock
From Africa to Europe / Through Africa to Europe
Fucking as an adjective:
Fucking hell! Fucking bastard. Fucking bitch. This fucking machine. Where is the fucking phonebook? I haven't a fucking clue.
Fucking as an adverb:
It is fucking cold. This is a fucking great idea. It is fucking brilliant. Don't be fucking stupid. A fucking awful film. You fucking well know what I mean.
Fucker, fuck all, fuck it, fuck me!

conversations.title: Lesson 123: Expressions - Goose bumps, I love you, Driving at, Pain
conversations.content:
Goose bumps = Goose flesh = Goose pimples. To make somebody's flesh crawl. To have the jimjams. To give somebody goose bumps. To give somebody the heebie-jeebies. To give somebody the collywobbles.
I love you. I love you so much. I dote on you. I am keen on you. I am fond of you. You are the apple of my eye.
To be driving at = to be getting at.
That's what I am getting at. What are you getting at? I see what you're driving at. I see what you're getting at.
Sore:
I have a sore head. I have a sore throat. I am sore all over. I have the menses.
Ache:
I have a headache, a bellyache, a stomachache, a backache. To ache. It is aching. My leg is aching. To pain. It is paining.
The most:
The most important thing, the most famous person, the most used verb.
To be in good terms with somebody. To be in bad terms with somebody.
Don't beat around the bush = Don't beach around the bush. Go straight to the point.
To be uncombed = To be unkempt = To be dishevelled = To be scruffy.
Pregnancy. To be pregnant. To be in the family way. To impregnate.
Bondage = Slavery. A slave. Triangle trade. Thralldom = Thrall.
Pet peeve = Pet hate = Pet aversion. To be somebody's pet peeve. Example: It is my pet aversion. It is our pet hate.

conversations.title: Lesson 124: Expressions - Well dressed, Worthy, Worrying, Behalf, Beyond, So-called
conversations.content:
To be well dressed, to be well bred.
Worthy: noteworthy, blameworthy, trustworthy, reliable. God is praiseworthy.
What is worrying you? What is bothering you? It bothers me = It worries me.
On the behalf of: on the behalf of my institute, on the behalf of my friends, on the behalf of everybody. To come on the behalf of... To speak on the behalf of...
Beyond:
It is beyond his means. It is beyond my abilities. It is beyond my reach. Beyond next week. Beyond next June. That is beyond human understanding.
So-called = supposedly:
A so-called poet = a would-be poet. A so-called teacher = a would-be teacher. He supposedly left for Rome. It would appear that = apparently.
Make:
To make a living, to make an appointment, to make a mistake, to make the difference, to make it clear, to make it, to make a decision, to make something of yourself. What make of car does he drive?
The command = The mastery. You have the command of English. She has the mastery of Chinese.
Clever = Smart = Bright = Intelligent = Skillful = Brilliant = Knowledgeable = Genius = Wise.
To be a big frog in a small pond = To be a big fish in a small pond.
I'd like to put the emphasis on... = I'd like to lay the emphasis on... = I'd like to focus on... = I'd like to be based on... = I'd like to be centered on...

conversations.title: Lesson 125: Expressions - Addict, Give a slip, Right hand, Put in place, Minimum, Miser
conversations.content:
Addict: a drug addict, a TV addict, a chocolate addict. To be addicted to. He is addicted to cigarettes. He is addicted to drugs.
To give somebody a slip. Example: You gave me a slip.
She is my right-handed friend. She is right-handed. She is left-handed.
To put somebody in his place. To take somebody down a peg. To tell somebody off. To be blunt to somebody. Example: I told him bluntly that...
At a minimum = At least. At the maximum = At the most.
To be miser = To be niggard = To be mean = To be selfish.
Hood as a suffix (state or quality of): falsehood, adulthood, sisterhood, childhood, motherhood, brotherhood, fatherhood, parenthood, boyhood, girlhood, widowhood, manhood, womanhood, priesthood, kinghood, selfhood, neighbourhood.
To apologize:
I apologize to cut you off. I apologize for the delay. I apologize for the fact that I am late. She apologizes for her insolence.
Know + how + to:
I know how to do it. I know how to sing. I don't know how to speak English. He doesn't know how to get by in English.
To be captivating = To be fascinating = To be seductive = To be charming.
Each other = One another (reflexive verbs):
To know each other, to love each other, to share each other, to help each other.
To introduce oneself (with reflexive pronouns):
I introduce myself, you introduce yourself, he introduces himself, she introduces herself, it introduces itself, we introduce ourselves, you introduce yourselves, they introduce themselves.

conversations.title: Lesson 126: Verbs with One's, Suffixes - Stricken, Struck, Clure
conversations.content:
To do one's best to:
I do my best to understand, you do your best to understand, he does his best to understand, she does her best to understand, it does its best to understand, we do our best to understand, you do your best to understand, they do their best to understand.
Stricken / Struck (touched by, struck by):
Plague-stricken, grief-stricken, panic-stricken, horror-stricken, poverty-stricken, awestruck, dumbstruck, thunderstruck.
Clure verbs:
Occlude, include, exclude, conclude.
To be pot bellied = To be pot belly.
Verbs that take 'have' in English (not 'be' like in French):
I have gone, I have come, she has come to discuss with you.
When 'be' is used, it means something else: I am finished.
Words with 4 's' letters: the first two 's' are pronounced 'z':
to dispossess, dispossession, to possess, possessions, possessive.
Go + and + verb (showing purpose):
I will go and fetch my purse. Go and get me a drink. Come and take it.
In North American English, 'and' is sometimes left out: Go ask your mom!
Goer (person who regularly goes to a place):
A church-goer, cinema-goer, movie-goer, film-goer, theatre-goer, play-goer, opera-goer.

conversations.title: Lesson 127: For + pronoun + to, When it comes to, Stop doing vs Stop to do
conversations.content:
For + object pronoun + to (so that):
For me to speak English, I must work hard. For you to speak English, you must work hard.
For (to show who can or should do something):
It is not for me to say why he left. It is for you to decide.
When it comes to + verb+ing:
When it comes to eating, he is the first. But when it comes to working, he dislikes it.
Not to (in order not to):
I decided not to do it. Not to disappoint you, I prefer you to drop it.
To stop doing something (to no longer do something):
We stopped taking pictures. (We were no longer taking pictures)
To stop to do something (to stop what you are doing to start doing something else):
We stopped to take pictures. (We stopped what we were doing to start taking pictures)
To gain one's living = To earn one's living.
Aren't we? Aren't you? Aren't they?
To be jammy = To be fortunate = To be lucky. A lucky charm.
To cue somebody = To give somebody a shout = To let somebody know = To motion to somebody to do something.
Can you cue me when you want me to begin speaking? Give me a shout when you're ready. Let me know when you're ready. Motion to me to do it when you're ready.
Incumbent president = Outgoing president. Counterpart.
Incubus (night husband), Succubus (night woman), Mermaid, Merman.
A boy wonder, a girl wonder. Attaboy! Attagirl!
Students' union, trade union, labour union.
Fatherland = Motherland = Homeland = Homeland.
Female students, female friends, female artists, female drivers.
Male students, male friends, male artists, male drivers.
It is strange = It is odd = It is queer.
It's being whispered that... = By hearsay.
To be stubborn = To be intransigent = To be headstrong = To be pig-headed.

conversations.title: Lesson 128: Suffixes - Ship, Maniac, Phone, Phobe, Phobia, Philia
conversations.content:
Suffix -ship:
Dictatorship, directorship, managership, championship, partnership, entrepreneurship.
Suffix -maniac:
Dipsomania (dipsomaniac), kleptomania (kleptomaniac), pyromania (pyromaniac).
Suffix -phone:
Anglophone (English-speaking), Arabophone (Arabic-speaking), Francophone (French-speaking), Germanophone (German-speaking), Hispanophone (Spanish-speaking), Italophone (Italian-speaking), Lusophone (Portuguese-speaking), Rusophone (Russian-speaking).
Suffix -phobe / -phobia:
Anglophobe (Anglophobia), Arabophobe (Arabophobia), Francophobe (Francophobia), Germanophobe (Germanophobia), Hispanophobe (Hispanophobia), Lusophobe (Lusophobia), Rusophobe (Rusophobia).
Suffix -philia:
Anglophile (Anglophilia), Francophile (Francophilia), Germanophile (Germanophilia), Hispanophile (Hispanophilia), Lusophile (Lusophilia), Rusophile (Rusophilia), haemophilia (haemophilic), necrophilia, pedophilia.
Suffix -phonie:
Anglophonie (French-speaking world), Lusophonie (Portuguese-speaking world).

conversations.title: Lesson 129: Words with Two Pronunciations (American vs British)
conversations.content:
Derby: American [darbi], British [derbi]
Director: American [dairekter], British [direkter]
Direction: American [dairekchen], British [direkchen]
Dilemma: American [dailema], British [dilema]
Idyll: American [aidol], British [idol]
Idyllic: American [naider], British [nider]
Leisure: American [leger], British [liger]
Either: American [aider], British [ider]
Neither: American [naider], British [nider]
Viral: American [vairol], British [virol]
Adjectives with 'hard':
hard-bitten (endured), hard-boiled (tough), hard-earned (hard-earned), hard-headed (realistic), hard-hearted (hard), hard-pressed / hard-pushed (in difficulty).

To have the right to + verb:
I have the right to study. My parents have the right to send me to school and to feed me. It is my right to ask anything of my parents. It is your right to educate your children.
Token (symbol of a feeling, fact, event):
Please accept this small gift as a token of our esteem, consideration, gratitude, respect.
Prefix re-:
To retry, to redo, to reuse, to reassess, to restart, to rebegin, to reappraise, to reshape, to resent, to restate, to reset, to resit.
A leader: The chief = The boss = The honcho.
Bursary = Scholarship = Grant. A bursar = A scholar = A grant holder.
To register (registration, registration fees). To enrol (enrolment, enrolment fees, enrollee). Tuition fees.

conversations.title: Tremendous Dialogues - Dialogue 5: There is / There are / There will be
conversations.content:
Can you tell me more about the different uses of 'il y a', 'il y avait', and 'il y aura'?
Yes I can
In the Present tense, we use 'there is' in singular and 'there are' in plural
Example: There is a show tonight. There are newcomers.
In the Past tense, we use 'there was' in singular and 'there were' in plural
Example: There was a traffic jam last night. There were foreigners at the airport.
In the Future tense, we use 'there will be' in singular and plural
Example: There will be many people at church. There will be rain this evening.

conversations.title: Tremendous Dialogues - Dialogue 6: Indefinite Article - A and An
conversations.content:
What's the difference between 'a' and 'an'?
The difference is: We use 'a' before consonants. Example: a boy, a girl.
Whereas, we use 'an' before vowels. Example: an animal, an apple.

conversations.title: Tremendous Dialogues - Dialogue 7: Difference between At and To
conversations.content:
What's the dichotomy between 'at' and 'to'?
We use 'at' if there is no movement. Examples: I am at church. I am at home. I am at work.
We use 'to' if there is movement. Examples: I am going to church. I am going to school. I am going to the city center. I am going to my friend's place.

conversations.title: Tremendous Dialogues - Dialogue 8: Possessive Case
conversations.content:
When do we use the possessive case?
The possessive case is mainly used to express possession or ownership.
When the name of the possessor is singular, we put apostrophe + 's' between the possessor and the possessed object. Formula: Possessor + 's + possessed object
Examples: Greg's house, My father's office
When the name of the possessor is plural, we put apostrophe after 's'. Examples: Gregs' house, Our friends' mother.

First way:
It's three o'clock
It's three sharp
It's ten past / after three
It's a quarter past / after three
It's half past / after three
To express the contrary of time, we use 'to': It's ten to four. It's a quarter to four.
Second way (clearer):
It's three, ten minutes
It's eleven, fifteen minutes
It's one, thirty minutes

conversations.title: Tremendous Dialogues - Sam and Willy
conversations.content:
What's up Sam?
Everything is hunky dory
Did you study your lessons yesterday?
Yes, I did
What did you study yesterday?
Yesterday, I studied the dialogue about religion
What did you understand by studying this dialogue?
Yesterday, by studying this dialogue, firstly I learned many things concerning religion. Secondly, I understood that there exists the Trinity. Besides, I can say that I am now able to ask questions regarding religion. In summary, I rejoice to be able to talk about religion without any problem.
What else did you study?
I also studied the difference between 'for', 'since' and 'ago'. I understood that there is a big difference between the three words. For instance, 'for' is always followed by cardinal numbers to talk about duration. However, 'since' is never followed by cardinal numbers. And finally, 'ago' is used to talk about an action that has already ended. Moreover, 'ago' is always placed at the end of a sentence.
What more did you discover?
I discovered many expressions like 'I am fed up!' and so on. And I also discovered words like 'nut' which means 'noix' in French.
Have you got something to add?
Yes, I have. I noticed that reading is the best thing; it opens the mind. When you read a lot, you will discover a lot of new words. Reading is the key element of education. To quote Nelson Mandela, 'education is the most powerful weapon'.
Is that all?
Yes, of course it is.
Congratulations! I'm actually satisfied that you have read and understood your lessons. I'm filled with joy. Break a leg! And keep your pecker up! Keep on working in the same way. I enjoy your way of working; I count on you. I pray that you will be a seed for your family. And I'm sure that with this way of working, you'll never disappoint your teachers and parents. God bless you again and again.
Thanks a lot. Cheerio!

conversations.title: Tremendous Dialogues - Choma and Roger
conversations.content:
Hi Roger, could I talk to you on the sly?
Of course, you could go on.
Well, I have a concern. I really have the desire to learn English. I am extremely eager to speak the language. I have a lot of problems with listening, hearing, writing, understanding, and especially speaking. I want you to help me, because I notice that you have a lot of advantages. I didn't know you like this. You didn't know anything concerning English, but now you can express yourself fluently in English. I am jealous of you now, you know? Not in a bad way. In fact, I wish to speak like you because you speak just like the natives themselves. Let me tell you that whenever you start speaking English, I feel embarrassed and automatically I look like a lost person. I want to emphasize that the lack of English knowledge in my life today represents a big gap, not only in my studies but also in my whole life. And when I also think of the fact that currently English intervenes in almost all domains of human activity, I am personally shocked. Reason why, I'd like you to tell me in which center you learned English. I have to learn it as quickly as possible, otherwise I will have problems in my future work.
So are you trying to say that you really expect to know English within two months? I am learning English at WEC-BEC, the only place I can advise you to go and learn English. Don't you see me? I have already changed in just a short time. Don't be astonished. It is easy. Just go and enroll your name; you will get the result.
I want to be a member of your center. What are the criteria? How much am I going to prepare for the enrollment? And what about the tuition fees? And ultimately, what can I do in order to get into contact with your head?
The registration fees are 2000 CFA. As for the tuition fees, we pay 5000 CFA per month. It is very cheap. The price is affordable for everyone, and our head's phone number is 05.562.98.20.
This time, man! I swear, I won't betray you. And what strategy can I apply or use in order to know English very rapidly?
Good question. First, you have to be courageous and you must like reading by making it your hobby, and ask questions to your teacher whenever you don't understand. And also you have to ask questions to those who already know the language. You must also be documented.
Thank you for your advice.

Why?
My boyfriend has betrayed me. He told me and promised me that he will always be faithful to me. And I have always been faithful to him too. He left home earlier in the morning, and since then, he is not yet back. I don't know why. Why me? Why me?
Are you serious? You feel like fainting just because of a boy? Don't you know that men are chameleons? Are you crying just because your husband has gone out?
Yes
Have you caught him with another woman?
Never trust a man entirely.
You know, my dear friend, jealousy is good if you love somebody. But sometimes if you're too jealous, it becomes so boring.
(Knock knock)
Talk of angels and you will hear the flutter of their wings. It is your husband who is coming.
Yes, come in.
What's happening here?
Your wife is grumbling because you missed her. You left home since the morning. That's the reason why she thinks that it is a kind of betrayal. Imagine, since you left, she couldn't reach you. Your phone is not working. I think she is right.
My bad girl, I apologize. I am so sorry. I have a serious crew meeting. You remember, we have a love covenant. Stop moaning. I am yours forever. I am worried if you complain. I recognize my fault. I will not repeat it. I have a refreshment for you. There is juice and coke. Choose your taste.

conversations.title: Part III - 1. Powerful Vocabulary: British and American English (Pronunciation)

2. I want to speak English (British) vs I wanna speak English (American)
3. I need some water (British) vs I need some water (American)
4. Words with 't' pronounced differently: letter, liter, later, meter, matter, little, title, subtle, subtile
5. I have got to speak English (British) vs I gotta speak English fluently (American)
6. center, internet, percentage, advantage, international (British) vs center (American)
7. to produce, to introduce, to seduce, to deduce (similar in both)
8. intervention, prevention, invention, attention, intention, contention, convention, detention, mention, expansion, extension, dimension, potential, financial, influential, residential, presidential, essential (British vs American slight variations)
9. on this day, in these days, in their house (similar in both)

conversations.title: Part III - 2. English Practice Section: How to Lead an English Club
conversations.content:
Opening remarks:
Hi ladies and gentlemen! / Hi folks!
First of all, I'd like to introduce myself, my name is... I am the MC of today's activities.
Therefore, I'd like someone to pray for the beginning of our activities.
Let's pray! / Shall we pray! / Let's praise the Lord! / Let's applaud for God!
Thank you, my brother/sister, for praying.
Introduction activity:
Let's move on to the next activity which is introduction. For this activity, I need everybody to introduce themselves. A big clap for everybody!
Dialogue activity:
Let's go on to the next activity which is the dialogue. For this activity, I need two persons in order to practice it. Let's clap for them. Go back and have a seat / Go back and sit down. I need another group for the same activity.
Interview activity:
Let's shift to the interview. For this activity, I invite four persons to come to practice it. Let's give them a big round of applause, that's great! I need another group.
Interpreting activity:
Let's come on to the activity of interpreting. I need two volunteers for practicing it.
Closing remarks:
Thank you for your attention! / Thank you for your best understanding!
My task is over; I need someone else to replace me. / My job is over; I need someone else to take over or to take the lead.
