conversations.title: Part III - 5. The Y.E.S Creed (Opening Remarks)
conversations.content:
Motto: Don't be passive! Be active! Be proactive! Be innovative! Be creative! WEC-BEC: Language is the tool of communication! We learn English to communicate.
Opening remarks by the President or representative:
Hi everyone! I am the President of WEC-BEC or the representative of the President. On behalf of the committee of WEC-BEC, I would like to thank you all. In fact, we are so privileged, honored, proud and ecstatic to see you coming to attend our club together with us. It is a phenomenal and monumental day for us. We bid you welcome. Welcome to our tremendous and awesome English club. This is a Foreign Languages Training Club. This is a Standard English Language Training Academy. The Youth English Service. It is a think-tank. WEC-BEC is our precious pearl and gem, our jewel, the cornerstone, and the Elysian fields. Our great white hope. WEC-BEC is a mascot and lucky charm for you and me. It is the blessed learning environment for the youth of our country in particular and Africa as a whole. We always achieve far-reaching and incredible results at WEC-BEC. Our center is a wizardry. Great things happen here, guys. Believe me. Let's get down to the nitty-gritty.
English is the backbone of our success. It is the bedrock. We are open to everyone. This is your home. We are the queen bee. It is not a secret; we are the Super English Club, and we are boundless. We strive to achieve success by the sweat of our brows. We are at the service of the English world. We are unbiased. We fight for justice and democracy, equal dignity and treatment. Our main challenge is to speak English fluently like the natives themselves. Our ultimate goal is interpreting and translation. It is time to translate words into action. Come out of your shell. Neither be afraid nor ashamed. You can't aim to speak this language fluently without making mistakes. No one is flawless. To err is human. The more you practice, the more you'll improve your level. Never neglect practice and research as a learner.
To those who take chances, to those who sacrifice themselves for this language, you will be rewarded somehow. We would specifically like to pay homage to every man and woman who usually visit OYAMB'S INSTITUTE every Saturday and Sunday. We are entirely grateful to you. We owe you much respect. You didn't make any mistake to come here. This is a legendary club. It is a legend. The righteousness of God is with you. You'll familiarize yourselves by dint of coming here. Thank you for the covenant you have made with the English language. You'll always benefit from our unswerving support. The longest journey begins with the first step. Let's follow the footsteps of our predecessors, among others William Shakespeare and Nelson Mandela, who had worked hard in this field to make our dreams come true.
Let's take conscious control of our own future. Let's strive for the spread of English across all our country. Let's promote this beautiful language. English will catapult you to fame. Remember, we are the leaders and ambassadors of our country and families. We are the icons and the architects of our lives. Trust me, this language offers a lot of assets. We're always available and ready to reach out to English language lovers.
We are doing all this in an effort to raise your awareness about the essence of English in our lives. We encourage your unshakeable commitment to learn this worldwide language.
You are the bookworms, the eggheads, the masterminds, the wunderkinds, and the kingpins. You are the salt of the earth. You are the leaders of tomorrow, and eventually, you have the Midas touch. You'll solve great issues with your English. You'll be the bigwigs. Actually, English is extremely so needed for you currently.
Dear country fellowmen, feel free and be comfortable. Don't be flabbergasted. We work professionally here. This is the galaxy. This is the paradise. Meaning, the El Dorado. Don't take what I am saying for granted. You who still joke with the practice of English, make no mistake. I stake my life that English will definitely open your doors, willy-nilly. Like it or not. It is undeniable. If you know English better in Congo here, you will impact people with this language. Let's change our mindset and mentality. Let's not be stubborn. Let's not be absent-minded. Let's carry out our tasks diligently and appropriately. Let's be faithful to our commitments. Do the best you possibly can in any kind of work you will get involved. Distinguish yourself in your work.
Dear guests, take note of this. We are super happy to receive you in our club, and we would like you to sit down suitably and harmoniously. Please, if you come late, don't greet people, just get in and take a seat quietly. Basically, life is a choice. Yes, a choice, because you have made a decision to come to practice at WEC-BEC club. We urge you to come to practice because we do know that the club is the best place that will help you develop your speaking, writing, reading, and listening skills. Whether you believe it or not. No pain, no gain. Your deeds shall speak for you and defend you. Just work tirelessly. Be headstrong. Get involved.
At WEC-BEC, you'll acquire the following values: Ability, Credibility, Credentials, Knowledge, Skills, Potentiality, Proficiency, Fluency, Wisdom, Intelligence, Fame, Respect, and a Qualification. We are perpetual learners. Just be careful. It's gonna be heated today. You're gonna enjoy it.
To all the guests, we'd like to let you know that you are all our VIPs today. Endeavor to succeed to speak English despite all the circumstances or setbacks that you'll encounter. It is a milestone we have achieved. WEC-BEC - Born leaders have inborn potentialities.

Art = are; hath = has; thy = your; thou = you; thine = yours; thee = you; sayeth = says; speaketh = speaks; prayeth = prays; giveth = gives

conversations.title: Annex Section - Singular and Plural of Nouns
conversations.content:
Regular plurals: add 's' (book-books, table-tables, shirt-shirts)
Add 'es' to nouns ending in sh, ch, x, s, z: brush-brushes, torch-torches, box-boxes, boss-bosses, quiz-quizzes
Replace 'y' with 'ies' for nouns ending with consonant+y: sky-skies, fly-flies, baby-babies
If a vowel precedes 'y', just add 's': key-keys, monkey-monkeys
Nouns ending in 'f' or 'fe' change to 'ves': calf-calves, half-halves, wife-wives

conversations.title: Annex Section - Punctuation
conversations.content:
1. Full stop (GB) / period (USA): (.)
2. Question mark: (?)
3. Exclamation mark: (!)
4. Comma: (,)
5. Semi-colon: (;)
6. Colon: (:)
7. Dash: (-)
8. Brackets: ()
9. Square brackets: []
10. Apostrophe: (')
11. Hyphen: (-)
12. Slash: (/)
13. Dots / ellipsis: (...)
14. Inverted commas / quotation marks: (" ") / (" ")

conversations.title: Annex Section - The Difference Between As and Like
conversations.content:
As - conjunction and adverb: As + subject + verb
Examples: Do as you like/want. As I said. Write this sentence exactly as it is written.
As can also be used before a preposition: It happened as in a dream.
Like - preposition, used before a noun
Examples: She is like her sister. I am not like my mother.
Note: In informal English, 'like' is often used as a conjunction/adverb instead of 'as': Nobody eats like I do. Like I have said.
'Like' is also used instead of 'as if' or 'as though' (meaning 'as if')

conversations.title: Annex Section - Emphasis in English
conversations.content:
Using 'Do', 'Does', 'Did' for emphasis (meaning 'really', 'indeed'):
1. I do feel sorry for Roger.
2. I do love you with all of my heart.
3. We do enjoy what you are doing.
4. It does seem strange.
5. She does love me.
6. He does count on his parents.
7. He did tell us about it.
8. I did talk to him with the naked eye.
9. We did agree to do it.
Note: The verb after 'do, does, did' remains in the infinitive without 'to'.
'Do' used in front of an imperative when urging someone to do something:
1. Do help yourself.
2. Do have a chocolate biscuit.
3. Do come on Sunday, you're gonna discover new things.

conversations.title: Annex Section - Money Currency
conversations.content:
In English-speaking countries, money symbols are put before the number, but read after it.
English countries currency:
£50: fifty pounds
$234: two hundred and thirty-four dollars
R1,250: one thousand two hundred and fifty rand
N500: five hundred naira
Other languages:
250 Y: two hundred and fifty yen
1,525 CFA: one thousand five hundred and twenty-five CFA
€2,000: two thousand Euro

conversations.title: Annex Section - Irregular and Regular Verbs
conversations.content:
Difference between irregular and regular verbs:
Irregular verbs have no rule and must be memorized: give-gave-given, go-went-gone, let-let-let, cut-cut-cut, cast-cast-cast
Regular verbs have the same preterit and past participle ending in 'ed': punish-punished-punished, work-worked-worked, study-studied-studied
Pronunciation of 'ed':
'id' after t,d: provide-provided-provided, disappoint-disappointed-disappointed
't' after c, ss, s, k, p, f, ch, sh, x: walk-walked-walked, laugh-laughed-laughed
'd' after other consonants (g, l, m, n, r, v, b, y, w): live-lived-lived, evolve-evolved-evolved, change-changed-changed
Note: Verbs ending with 'y' preceded by a consonant take 'ied' in preterit and past participle, and 'ies' at third person singular: study-studied-studied, try-tried-tried, cry-cried-cried, carry-carried-carried, dry-dried-dried, fry-fried-fried

conversations.title: Annex Section - Different Types of Verbs
conversations.content:
I. Verbs not used in progressive tenses:
Forget, Remember, Mean, See, Seem, Hear, Like, Love, Hate, Know, Believe, Prefer, Want, Understand, Notice, Belong, Realize, Need.
II. Verbs that are irregular in British English and regular in American English:
1. learn - learnt - learnt (GB) / learn - learned - learned (US)
2. spoil - spoilt - spoilt (GB) / spoil - spoiled - spoiled (US)
3. spell - spelt - spelt (GB) / spell - spelled - spelled (US)
4. smell - smelt - smelt (GB) / smell - smelled - smelled (US)
5. burn - burnt - burnt (GB) / burn - burned - burned (US)
6. dream - dreamt - dreamt (GB) / dream - dreamed - dreamed (US)
7. dwell - dwelt - dwelt (GB) / dwell - dwelled - dwelled (US)
8. kneel - knelt - knelt (GB) / kneel - kneeled - kneeled (US)
9. leap - leapt - leapt (GB) / leap - leaped - leaped (US)
10. lean - leant - leant (GB) / lean - leaned - leaned (US)
11. spill - spilt - spilt (GB) / spill - spilled - spilled (US)
III. Verbs of perception: To hear, to see, to look, to listen, to feel, to smell, to taste
It is more common to say: I can hear you, I can see you (using 'can' with these verbs)
IV. Linking verbs followed by gerund: to like, to dislike, to start, to begin, to keep, to stop, to enjoy, to prefer - followed by verb+ing
V. Linking verbs (current and resulting):
Current: to be, to look, to appear, to lie, to remain, to seem, to stay, to smell, to sound, to state
Examples: She seems a cute girl. Your house looks awfully nice. He becomes very kind. It sounds good.
Resultant: to become, to grow, to fall, to run, to turn, to get

conversations.content:

We go to church to pray.
We go to college to study.
We go to bed to sleep.
We go to school to study.
We go to sea as sailors.
We go to prison as prisoners.
We go to court as litigants.
We go to work as workers.
Go to the + place - when going for other reasons (e.g., visiting someone):
I am going to the school to pay the tuition.
She goes to the church to see how people rehearse.

conversations.title: Annex Section - The Use of Large Cardinal Numbers
conversations.content:
For numbers over 100, use 'and' to link hundreds and tens:
103 = one hundred and three
3,248 = three thousand two hundred and forty-eight
For numbers over 1000, use 'and' only when the number after 1000 is less than 100. When the number after 1000 is more than 100, 'and' is not used:
1,852 = one thousand, eight hundred and fifty-two
1,040 = one thousand and forty
Examples:
503,609 = five hundred and three thousand six hundred and nine
4,384,511 = four million three hundred and eighty-four thousand five hundred and eleven

638,249,341 = six hundred and thirty-eight million two hundred and forty-nine thousand three hundred and forty-one
25,830,975,532 = twenty-five billion eight hundred and thirty million nine hundred and seventy-five thousand five hundred and thirty-two

conversations.title: Annex Section - African Civilization and English-Speaking Countries in Africa
conversations.content:
Apartheid: the former political system in South Africa in which only white people had full political rights and other people, especially black people, were forced to live away from white people, go to separate schools, etc. Apartheid was a political, socio-economic system based on the principle of separate development for every race in South Africa. Apartheid is racial discrimination or a separate development between races. Apartheid is racial segregation. Apartheid aimed at establishing a white nation in the black continent. Apartheid takes its roots far back in history, almost in the 17th century.
English-speaking countries in Africa:
Nigeria, Ethiopia, Ghana, Uganda, Kenya, Tanzania, Zambia, Malawi, Zimbabwe, Botswana, Namibia, South Africa, Cameroon, Gambia, Sierra Leone, Liberia
Powerful English countries: The United Kingdom, The United States of America, Canada, New Zealand
Great Britain is made up of 4 countries:
1. England - capital: London
2. Scotland - capital: Edinburgh
3. Wales - capital: Cardiff
4. Northern Ireland - capital: Belfast
The United Kingdom (U.K.) is ruled by a monarch. The British Monarchy is known as a constitutional monarchy. As Head of State, the monarch undertakes constitutional and representational duties. The monarch has limited power.
Role of the Queen: Along with the House of Lords, Queen Elizabeth plays a constitutional role in opening and dissolving parliament and approving bills before they become law. She is Britain's head of state, but her executive powers are limited.
Role of the Prime Minister (PM): The Prime Minister of the UK is the head of the government. The PM directs both executive powers and legislative powers, including powers over parliament, powers over his or her political party, and powers of appointment.
The Congress is made of the House of Commons and the House of Lords.
Origin of English language: The name 'England' is derived from the Old English name Englaland, which means 'Land of the Angles.' The Angles were one of the Germanic tribes that settled in Great Britain during the Early Middle Ages.
The United States (U.S.) has 50 states. The U.S.A. has currently two main political parties: The Democratic Party (the Left) and the Republican Party (the Right). The Swing States (also The Key States) are: Pennsylvania, Wisconsin, New Hampshire, Minnesota, Arizona, Georgia, Virginia, Florida, Michigan, Nevada, Colorado, North Carolina, Maine.

conversations.title: Annex Section - The United States Oaths
conversations.content:
The Vice President's oath:
I, (name), do solemnly swear (or affirm) that I will support and defend the Constitution of the United States against all enemies, foreign and domestic; that I will bear true faith and allegiance to the same; that I take this obligation freely, without any mental reservation or purpose of evasion; and that I will well and faithfully discharge the duties of the office on which I am about to enter. So help me God.
The Presidential oath:
I, (name), do solemnly swear (or affirm) that I will faithfully execute the office of President of the United States, and will to the best of my ability, preserve, protect and defend the Constitution of the United States. So help me God.
The Capitol Hill: The headquarters of the Congress in the U.S.
The Oval Office: The official office of the U.S. President.
The White House: The official home and workplace of the President of the USA.
The Pentagon: The headquarters of the U.S. Department of Defense.
The Statue of Liberty.
The American flag is made up of 13 stripes and 50 stars.
American greatest firms: Microsoft (Bill Gates), Amazon (Jeff Bezos), Google (Larry Page and Sergey Brin), Apple (Steve Jobs), Facebook (Mark Zuckerberg), Twitter (Jack Dorsey), Exxon Mobil, Walmart, YouTube (Jawed Karim, Steve Chen, Chad Hurley), WhatsApp (Jan Koum and Brian Acton), Instagram (Kevin Systrom).

conversations.title: Annex Section - Tertiary Education and The Ivy League
conversations.content:
Graduation: The ceremony of university diploma presentation. It is an important event where students wearing their black gowns and mortarboards officially receive their diplomas from the chancellor.
Cadet: A student at a military school who is training to be an officer.
The Ivy League: A group of eight old and respected universities in the USA. The Ivy League schools are considered to be the most prestigious of all colleges in the United States.
The eight Ivy League colleges: Brown, Harvard, Cornell, Princeton, Dartmouth, Yale, Columbia universities, and the University of Pennsylvania.

conversations.title: Annex Section - The USA National Anthem (The Star-Spangled Banner)
conversations.content:
O say can you see, by the dawn's early light,
What so proudly we hailed at the twilight's last gleaming,
Whose broad stripes and bright stars through the perilous fight,
O'er the ramparts we watched were so gallantly streaming?
And the rocket's red glare, the bombs bursting in air,
Gave proof through the night that our flag was still there,
O say does that star-spangled banner yet wave
O'er the land of the free and the home of the brave?
On the shore dimly seen through the mists of the deep,
Where the foe's haughty host in dread silence reposes,
What is that which the breeze, o'er the towering steep,
As it fitfully blows, half conceals, half discloses?
Now it catches the gleam of the morning's first beam,
In full glory reflected now shines in the stream,
'Tis the star-spangled banner - O long may it wave
O'er the land of the free and the home of the brave!
And where is that band who so vauntingly swore,
That the havoc of war and the battle's confusion,
A home and a country should leave us no more?
Their blood has washed out their foul footsteps' pollution.
No refuge could save the hireling and slave
From the terror of flight or the gloom of the grave,
And the star-spangled banner in triumph doth wave
O'er the land of the free and the home of the brave.
O thus be it ever when freemen shall stand
Between their loved home and the war's desolation!
Blest with victory and peace, may the heaven-rescued land
Praise the Power that hath made and preserved us a nation!
Then conquer we must, when our cause it is just,
And this be our motto - 'In God is our trust,'
And the star-spangled banner in triumph shall wave
O'er the land of the free and the home of the brave.

conversations.title: Annex Section - UK's National Anthem (God Save the Queen)
conversations.content:
God save our gracious Queen,
Long live our noble Queen,
God save the Queen.
Send her victorious,
Happy and glorious,
Long to reign over us,
God save the Queen.
O Lord our God arise,
Scatter her enemies,
And make them fall.
Confound their politics,
Frustrate their knavish tricks,
On thee our hopes we fix,
God save us all.
Thy choicest gifts in store,
On her be pleased to pour,
Long may she reign.
May she defend our laws,
And ever give us cause,
To sing with heart and voice,
God save the Queen.
Not in this land alone,
But be God's mercies known,
From shore to shore.
Lord make the nations see,
That men should brothers be,
And form one family,
The wide world over.
From every latent foe,
From the assassin's blow,
God save the Queen.
O'er her thine arm extend,
For Britain's sake defend,
Our mother, prince, and friend,
God save the Queen.
Lord grant that Marshal Wade
May by thy mighty aid
Victory bring.
May he sedition hush,
And like a torrent rush,
Rebellious Scots to crush,
God save the King.
